title: update_transfer_template cannot rotate package addr â€” post-upgrade DoS of PAS transfers
description: `register_transfer_template` derives the template's `package_id` from `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()`. Per `std::type_name` (see `/Users/alilloig/workspace/sui/external-crates/move/crates/move-stdlib/sources/type_name.move` L41-43 â€” "Package IDs that appear in fully qualified type names in the output from this function are defining IDs (the ID of the package in storage that first introduced the type)"), this is ALWAYS the package that first defined `TransferApproval` â€” i.e. the V1 hadron package. After a hadron upgrade, the autoresolve template still routes `approve_transfer<T>` to V1. V1's `hadron::version::check_is_valid` hardcodes `VERSION = 1` and asserts `self.version == VERSION`; once `version::migrate` bumps the shared `Version` to V2's constant, the V1 entry point aborts, breaking every PAS transfer. Worse, `update_transfer_template` re-runs the SAME defining-id resolution, so calling it does NOT rotate the address â€” the operator has no on-chain way to fix the template after migrate.
impact: Permanent DoS of all PAS transfers after any hadron upgrade. The documented recovery path (`update_transfer_template`) is a no-op; operators will discover this only after migration and will have no on-chain remedy short of re-publishing and re-registering the asset.
recommendation: Compute the package_id from a source that tracks the latest upgraded package. Options: (a) record the package address explicitly in the Treasury/Namespace at publish/upgrade time and read it here; (b) accept `package_id: String` as a parameter to `update_transfer_template` so the governance-signed operator provides the new address after migrate; (c) write the hadron V1/V2 `approve_transfer` functions to be version-tolerant enough (e.g. allow the function to be called at V1 even after migration if the logic is still semantically identical). Also fix the misleading doc-comment that claims the function is "Useful after package upgrades to point to the new package address."

title: update_transfer_template<T> lets any institution overwrite another institution's template
description: `update_transfer_template<T>` only checks that the caller's `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` is valid for the caller's own `treasury`. It does NOT assert that `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` â€” i.e. that coin T was actually registered in THIS treasury. Meanwhile the `Templates` object is a singleton shared across all hadron institutions (keyed by `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>()`, which is coin-independent-of-treasury). Consequence: Institution B â€” with any RegisterCoinPermission â€” can call `update_transfer_template<Foo>` for Institution A's coin `Foo`, and the new template will call `approve_transfer<Foo>(B_treasury, ..., version)`. Since B's treasury has no `WhitelistKey<Foo>` DOF, every legitimate transfer of Foo will abort inside `assert_can_send<Foo>` / `dynamic_object_field::borrow`.
impact: Any institution administrator in the hadron deployment can grief any other institution's PAS transfers. This is a cross-institution authorization-boundary break: per the Hadron Blueprint, institutions should be isolated.
recommendation: In `update_transfer_template<T>` (and `register_transfer_template` / `register_pas_asset`), assert `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` before writing the template. This ensures only the institution that originally registered T can update its template.

title: pas_supply::burn allows destroying any whitelisted-or-not account's balance without user consent
description: `burn` accepts a `Request<ClawbackFunds<Balance<T>>>` created by `pas::account::clawback_balance` (upstream `account.move` L99-106 â€” "This takes no Auth, as it's an admin action"). Any RedeemPermission holder can consume such a Request, approving `ClawbackApproval<T>` and burning the balance. There is no whitelist/ownership check on `from` and no separate permission distinguishing "user-initiated redemption" from "admin-initiated forced burn". The spec says `burn` "checks: not paused, amount > 0" â€” which matches the code but also matches what would be called a forced clawback-and-burn elsewhere. In practice this means a single RedeemPermission role can wipe any holder's balance without their co-signature.
impact: Design ambiguity. If the intent is "user redeemed off-chain, admin burns on-chain," then an additional off-chain attestation / on-chain user signature is assumed. If the intent is forced burn, this is a very strong authority with no audit trail distinguishing it from `seize`.
recommendation: Clarify the intended semantics. Options: (1) require the source address to be whitelisted to send (so de-whitelisted addresses must go through `seize`, preserving audit trail); (2) require a user-provided `pas::account::Auth` to gate user-requested redemptions, with a separate `force_burn` for admin clawbacks that carries its own permission; (3) if intentional, document the authority explicitly in the module doc and event.

title: Mint/redeem pause also disables PAS transfers â€” spec ambiguity between two distinct kill switches
description: `approve_transfer` asserts `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()` using the same `MintRedeemPausedKey<T>` flag that `supply::mint` / `supply::redeem` and `pas_supply::mint/burn/seize` gate on. Pausing mint/redeem therefore also pauses all user-to-user PAS transfers. There is no separate "pause transfers" switch, and there is no separate `PausePasTransferPermission`. A compliance officer who wants to stop new issuance while letting existing holders continue to transfer cannot express that intent.
impact: Operational rigidity and conflated kill switches. Emergency pausing of mint also freezes a market.
recommendation: Introduce a distinct per-coin pause state for PAS transfers (or gate `approve_transfer` on a `PasTransferPausedKey<T>` instead of reusing mint-redeem pause). Add matching `PausePasTransferPermission` / `UnpausePasTransferPermission` if granular control is desired.

title: pas_supply::burn/seize do not re-check version â€” only the auth-creation path does
description: `pas_supply::burn` and `pas_supply::seize` do not call `version.check_is_valid()` directly. They rely on the auth being freshly constructed in the same PTB (which does call `version.check_is_valid()` via `auth::new_auth`). This is usually fine because auths have `drop` and cannot be stored, but `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` DOES call `version.check_is_valid()` explicitly (line 38) â€” which creates an inconsistency. Moreover, because `Auth<P, T>` is `drop`-only and freshly minted, the guarantee holds, but future refactors that relax Auth's drop-only property would silently break the gate.
impact: Defensive consistency. Low risk today; fragile on future refactor.
recommendation: Either add `version.check_is_valid()` explicitly at the top of `pas_supply::mint/burn/seize`, OR document (via a module-level comment) that the version gate is delegated to auth creation. Similarly remove the explicit check in `approve_transfer` if the convention is to delegate, or add it everywhere.

title: WhitelistManagePermission on a non-PAS coin yields opaque abort in DOF borrow
description: `auth::new_auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` only asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()` (i.e. TreasuryCapKey<T> exists). That check is also satisfied for non-PAS coins registered via `admin::register_coin`. The caller can then build `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` and call `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist<T>` / `remove_from_whitelist<T>` / `set_whitelist_*`, which internally call `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut<WhitelistKey<T>>(...)` on a DOF that was never created. The transaction aborts deep inside sui-framework with an opaque "field does not exist" error rather than a clean `ENotPasCoin`.
impact: Developer ergonomics / observability. No security impact, but a legitimate operator mistake surfaces as a cryptic abort instead of `ENotPasCoin`.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of each whitelist mutator in `treasury.move` (`add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`), and similarly on the read helpers (`is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`) so callers fail with a clear error.

title: Move.toml pins `pas` and `ptb` to branch `main` â€” non-reproducible dependency
description: The `pas` and `ptb` dependencies use `rev = "main"`, which is a branch name, not a commit hash. Every fresh `sui move build` re-resolves to whatever `main` currently points to upstream. The `Move.lock` does pin to `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc` for the `testnet` environment, but the manifest drift means (a) builds in other environments will pick up different code, (b) `sui move manage-package` flows that ignore the lock will drift, and (c) upstream pushes to `main` can silently change the ABI assumed by Hadron (e.g., `send_funds::resolve_balance` semantics).
impact: Build non-reproducibility and silent ABI drift risk. Not itself exploitable, but a persistent class of supply-chain issues for any reviewer / auditor reproducing the build.
recommendation: Pin `rev` to the same commit hash recorded in `Move.lock` (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`). Update via governance-tracked PRs only. Ideally add a CI check that fails if manifest `rev` is not a 40-char hex string.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: Move.toml uses `edition = "2024.beta"` â€” prefer stable `2024`
description: Move 2024 is stable. `2024.beta` is the pre-stable identifier. The new `token_template_pas/Move.toml` (correctly) uses `edition = "2024"`, so the two packages are inconsistent. Some Move 2024 features or compiler behaviours may differ between the `beta` and stable editions.
impact: Tooling inconsistency; minor risk that `beta` edition behaviour diverges from stable.
recommendation: Change root `Move.toml` to `edition = "2024"` to match `token_template_pas/Move.toml` and current framework guidance.
evidence:
[package]
name = "hadron"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024.beta"

title: No tests for any PAS module (pas_admin, pas_supply, pas_transfer, treasury PAS caps)
description: `sources/pas_admin.move`, `sources/pas_supply.move`, and `sources/pas_transfer.move` contain zero `#[test]` / `#[test_only]` functions. The modified PAS-specific functions in `sources/treasury.move` (`add_pas_coin_caps`, `is_pas_coin`, whitelist table CRUD) have no test coverage either. All critical invariants (whitelist gating of mint/transfer/seize, PAS vs non-PAS coin segregation in supply::mint/redeem, template registration/refresh, multi-institution template overwrite) are unverified.
impact: Regressions on this PR or any follow-up are undetectable by CI. Integration-boundary breakage from upstream PAS changes will not be caught until production.
recommendation: Add module-level tests covering at minimum: (1) `register_pas_asset` happy path + double-registration abort, (2) `mint` / `burn` / `seize` happy path + amount-zero / paused / not-whitelisted aborts, (3) `approve_transfer` happy path + paused / unwhitelisted sender / unwhitelisted recipient aborts, (4) `supply::mint` / `supply::redeem` abort with `EPasCoinNotAllowed` when T is a PAS coin, (5) whitelist mutators: add+remove, add-already-present, remove-absent, set_send / set_receive on unknown address.

title: No tests for pas_supply::mint/burn/seize
description: Zero `#[test]` functions in `pas_supply.move`. The ClawbackApproval witness emission, amount>0 guards, and `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` gating on each entry point are untested. No test validates that `ClawbackApproval<T>` (defined here) matches the approval type registered in `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` via `policy.set_required_approval<_, ClawbackApproval<T>>`.
impact: Possible silent type-name mismatch between `set_required_approval` and `request.approve` would only surface at first real clawback in production. Integration-boundary contract is entirely unverified.
recommendation: Add tests using `sui::test_scenario` that exercise the full PAS flow (create namespace + treasury + PAS coin â†’ mint â†’ whitelist â†’ approve_transfer â†’ burn â†’ seize) against the upstream PAS package pinned in Move.lock.

title: No tests for pas_transfer::approve_transfer whitelist gating
description: The permissionless autoresolve entrypoint `approve_transfer` is the main compliance surface for user-to-user PAS transfers. It is untested. Critical paths unverified: (a) `ETransferAmountZero` abort; (b) `EMintRedeemPaused` abort; (c) `ENotWhitelistedSend` / `ENotWhitelistedReceive` when sender or recipient are not in whitelist or have their `can_send`/`can_receive` flag cleared; (d) correct `PasTransferApproved` event emission ordering; (e) request-approval type matches `TransferApproval<T>` as registered in policy.
impact: Compliance bypass bugs could ship undetected.
recommendation: Write unit + scenario tests for each abort branch listed above. Include a test that flips `can_send` / `can_receive` independently to verify the flags actually gate the right direction.

title: scripts/operations/pas.ts: `pkg()` silently returns '' if CONSTANTS unloaded
description: `pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`. Both default to empty string (lines 52-55: `deployed.package ?? { v1: '', latest: '' }`). If `deploy-output.json` is missing or malformed, every caller silently constructs move calls with `package: ''` â€” producing a Transaction that will fail at sign-time or publish-time with a vague validation error. No guard throws a human-readable error.
impact: Operator footgun; every PAS script will fail with a confusing error when deploy output is missing, instead of a clear "deploy-output.json not found or missing packageId" message.
recommendation: Throw a clear error in `pkg()` (or at module load) if both `latest` and `v1` are empty strings. Alternatively, have `pkg()` assert a 66-char hex prefix.

title: scripts/deployPasTokenTemplate: substring-match on object types is fragile
description: The code identifies TreasuryCap / MetadataCap / Currency from `deployResult.effects?.changedObjects` by string-containment check on the object's type string (e.g. `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`). This is brittle: (a) if the framework renames or namespaces these types in a minor release, the script silently returns empty IDs; (b) ambiguous matches (e.g. a user-defined type that contains `::coin::TreasuryCap<` in its name) would be mis-assigned; (c) first-match-wins iteration order depends on effects ordering. A more robust approach parses the generic argument and asserts the package address is `0x2`.
impact: Brittle deploy script; a cosmetic framework rename can break PAS token registration with no clear diagnostic.
recommendation: Use a regex / structured type parse that matches exactly `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<...>`, `0x2::coin_registry::MetadataCap<...>`, `0x2::coin_registry::Currency<...>` and asserts exactly one match per key. Surface an explicit error when matching produces 0 or >1 results.

title: scripts/deployPasTokenTemplate: coinType synthesized from symbol without OTW validation
description: The coinType is reconstructed as `${coinPackageId}::${symbol}::${otw}` where `symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toLowerCase()` and `otw = tokenValues.symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")`. This assumes the template's MODULE name matches `symbol.toLowerCase()` (it does for `hadron_bond` / `HBOND`? â€” actually `symbol` is `HBOND` lowercased to `hbond`, but the module name comes from bytecode patching and may be `moduleName: 'hadron_bond'`). The example invocation passes `{ moduleName: 'hadron_bond', symbol: 'HBOND', ... }` â€” symbol.lowercase() is `hbond`, which does NOT match `hadron_bond`. This inconsistency will produce an invalid coinType unless the patcher renames the module to match `symbol.toLowerCase()`, which is not obvious from the code here.
impact: Deploy script likely produces wrong coinType on realistic inputs, leading to downstream Move-call abort (coin type not registered).
recommendation: Derive coinType directly from the effects/type_metadata by finding the TreasuryCap<C> and using C. Alternatively, assert that `moduleName === symbol.toLowerCase()` at the start of `deployAndRegisterPasAsset`, or extract the module name from the patched bytecode rather than from user-supplied fields.

title: scripts: PAS mint amount typed as JS `number` â€” u64 overflow / precision hazard
description: `mintPasTokens(treasuryId, coinType, accountId, amount: number)` types `amount` as JS `number`. Move-side the amount is a `u64` (max ~1.8e19). JS `number` is IEEE-754 double with safe integer range only up to 2^53-1 (~9.0e15). Amounts > `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` will silently lose precision when BCS-serialized â€” and tokens use 6-decimal precision, so `1_000_000 * 1e6 = 1e12` fits today, but a malicious caller can pass `2^60` which will be serialized truncated. Same class of issue exists in example full-setup (100_000, 1_000_000 literals) and is not inherently broken, but the API should enforce bigint.
impact: Any future caller passing a large amount would silently corrupt it. Already a recurring foot-gun across the SDK ecosystem.
recommendation: Change the `amount` parameter to `bigint` in `mintPasTokens` (and `mintTokens` if phase-1 has the same issue â€” out of scope but worth flagging to the phase-1 reviewer). Serialize with `tx.pure.u64(amount)` and document the unit (smallest unit / decimals) in the JSDoc.

title: scripts/full-setup: SIGNER_1_PK parsed without null check â€” env-missing crash is unhelpful
description: `const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;` uses the non-null assertion (`!`) and then immediately calls `fromBase64(pk1B64).slice(1)`. If `SIGNER_1_PK` is unset, `pk1B64` is `undefined` and `fromBase64` will throw an unhelpful error. Inconsistent with `utils.ts::publicKeyFromEnv` which has a proper label-tagged error.
impact: Developer ergonomics. The example is the primary onboarding path.
recommendation: Either reuse `publicKeyFromEnv` from `utils.ts`, or add an explicit `if (!pk1B64) throw new Error('SIGNER_1_PK missing in .env')`.
evidence:
const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;
    const receiver = new Ed25519PublicKey(fromBase64(pk1B64).slice(1)).toSuiAddress();

title: scripts/deployPasTokenTemplate: bytecode dependencies omit CoinRegistry shared object
description: `tx.publish({ modules: [...], dependencies: [normalizeSuiObjectId('0x1'), normalizeSuiObjectId('0x2')] })` declares only `0x1` (std) and `0x2` (sui-framework) as publish-time deps. `sui::coin_registry` lives inside `0x2` so that is actually correct at module-dep level; however, the subsequent `finalize_registration` call in `pas.ts` passes `COIN_REGISTRY = '0xc'` as a shared object. This is a runtime-arg object (shared registry), not a package-level dep, so not strictly wrong â€” but the hard-coded `'0xc'` is opaque and should probably come from the framework SDK rather than be hand-written.
impact: Maintainability. If the shared registry address ever changes (unlikely), the scripts silently break.
recommendation: Source the CoinRegistry address from `@mysten/sui` constants (or from `SUI_CLOCK_OBJECT_ID`-style framework exports) rather than hardcoding `'0xc'`. Add a code comment referencing the framework doc at `references/sui-framework-reference.mdx`.

title: register_pas_asset lacks explicit is_pas_coin guard â€” opaque abort on double-registration
description: If `register_pas_asset<T>` is called twice for the same T (or after a non-PAS `admin::register_coin<T>`), the call sequence is: (1) `policy::new_for_currency<T>` aborts inside upstream with `EPolicyAlreadyExists` (policy.move L51), OR (2) succeeds (first PAS registration after a non-PAS registration) then Hadron's `add_pas_coin_caps` aborts with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` on the treasury_cap_key DOF. Either path aborts the txn (no state leak), but the local error surface is confusing â€” depending on call order the operator sees a PAS error code or a treasury error code.
impact: Minor UX; hard to diagnose misconfigurations.
recommendation: Add an early assert in `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` â€” e.g. `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered)` â€” so operators get one, predictable error code before any upstream policy state is touched.

title: WhitelistManagePermission collapses two spec permissions (WhitelistAdd, WhitelistRemove)
description: The PAS spec (per `_context.md` Â§7.2) defines distinct `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission`. The implementation uses a single `WhitelistManagePermission` for both add/remove and for `set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`. This is a deliberate simplification and aligns with the Hadron Blueprint "Permissions" naming, but any segregation-of-duty policy (e.g., "junior compliance officer can add, only senior can remove") is not expressible.
impact: Design drift from spec. Blocks finer-grained compliance role design.
recommendation: Either (a) leave as-is and update the spec / Notion to match code, or (b) split into `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` (and `WhitelistSetFlagsPermission`) if segregation-of-duty is on the roadmap.

title: WhitelistEntry has no rate-limit or expiry fields â€” uses booleans only
description: The PAS spec does not require expiry or rate-limit fields on whitelist entries, but the current design (two booleans: `can_send`, `can_receive`) is very coarse. There's no expiry, no daily/monthly transfer limit, and no audit trail beyond the `WhitelistAdded`/`WhitelistRemoved` events. For a compliance-grade regulated asset this is a thin model.
impact: Not a bug â€” observation for design review. Compliance regimes (KYC/AML) often demand transferrable limits with time bounds.
recommendation: Consider extending `WhitelistEntry` with an expiry timestamp and optional per-address send/receive caps, gated by the same `WhitelistManagePermission`. If the scope is intentional, document the rationale in the module doc.

title: pas_supply::mint: account.owner() is raw address â€” object accounts not explicitly documented
description: `mint` uses `account.owner()` as the whitelist key. Per upstream `account.move` L132-134, an `Account` may be owned by a `UID` (object) via `new_auth_as_object(uid)` â€” in that case `owner` is `uid.to_inner().to_address()`, a synthetic address. Whitelisting object-owned accounts therefore requires knowing that synthetic address. Not inherently wrong, but undocumented on the Hadron side.
impact: Potential integrator confusion when an object is the asset holder.
recommendation: Add a doc-comment on `pas_supply::mint` (and on whitelist mutators) explaining that for object-owned PAS accounts the whitelist must hold the object's derived address (`uid.to_inner().to_address()`).

title: supply.move::mint/redeem: `EPasCoinNotAllowed` ordering makes error messages less specific
description: In `supply::mint`, the assertion order is: (1) `amount > 0` â†’ `EMintAmountZero`, (2) `!is_pas_coin<T>()` â†’ `EPasCoinNotAllowed`, (3) `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Moving the PAS-coin check before the amount check would give better diagnostics (the PAS vs non-PAS mismatch is a programmer error; amount=0 is a user error). Minor.
impact: Diagnostic quality only.
recommendation: Reorder assertions: put `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinNotAllowed)` first so callers get a structural-error-code-early. Same for `redeem`.

title: events: PasBurned lacks the burner identity â€” audit trail incomplete
description: `PasBurned<T>` records `from: address` and `amount: u64` but does not record the admin address (i.e. `ctx.sender()`) that invoked `pas_supply::burn`. `Redeemed<T>` (non-PAS) does include `sender`. This inconsistency complicates admin-action audit: there's no on-chain way to distinguish which compliance officer executed a given forced burn.
impact: Compliance / audit traceability. Not security-critical, but regulatory reviews usually want to identify the acting admin.
recommendation: Add an `admin: address` (or `sender: address`) field to `PasBurned` and `PasSeized` and populate with `ctx.sender()` inside `pas_supply::burn` / `pas_supply::seize`. Also consider adding the auth'd permission type as a phantom marker for filter-ability.

title: treasury::add_pas_coin_caps does not destroy the unused DenyCap slot â€” DOF layout divergence
description: Non-PAS `add_coin_caps` adds three DOFs: `treasury_cap_key<T>`, `deny_cap_key<T>`, `metadata_cap_key<T>`. PAS `add_pas_coin_caps` adds `treasury_cap_key<T>`, `metadata_cap_key<T>`, `policy_cap_key<T>`, `whitelist_key<T>` â€” NO `deny_cap_key<T>`. This is by design (PAS uses whitelist not denylist), but means `is_coin_registered<T>` returns true for both paths while `deny_cap_mut<T>` would abort on PAS coins with `ECoinNotRegistered`. A caller that only knows `is_coin_registered<T>` cannot tell which cap scheme is in use.
impact: Surface ambiguity; no security impact today but any future code that assumes `is_coin_registered<T>` implies `deny_cap_mut<T>` is available will abort on PAS coins.
recommendation: Callers of `deny_cap_mut<T>` should first assert `!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` and return a clean `EPasCoinNotAllowed`-style error. Alternatively, expose `is_regulated_coin<T>()` as the counterpart to `is_pas_coin<T>()` for callers.

title: pas_transfer::transfer_approval_permit public(package) â€” no doc on internal::permit defining-module rule
description: `transfer_approval_permit<T>()` returns `internal::Permit<TransferApproval<T>>` and is marked `public(package)` so `pas_admin::register_transfer_template` can construct it. Per `std::internal` (see `/Users/alilloig/workspace/sui/external-crates/move/crates/move-stdlib/sources/internal.move` L34-38 â€” "Instances can only be created by the module that defines the type T"), only the module defining `TransferApproval` can call `internal::permit<TransferApproval<T>>()`. The doc-comment mentions this rule but without referencing the stdlib contract. A future refactor that moves `TransferApproval` to a different module will silently break the template-registration pathway.
impact: Refactor hazard. A naive rename could produce a cryptic compile error far from the actual issue.
recommendation: Expand the doc-comment to cite `std::internal::Permit`'s defining-module rule and to note that `TransferApproval` and `transfer_approval_permit` MUST remain in the same module. Add a similar note in `pas_supply` for `ClawbackApproval` even though it's not currently permitted externally.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset hardcodes clawback_allowed=true without doc justification
description: The call `policy::new_for_currency(pas_namespace, &mut treasury_cap, true)` passes `clawback_allowed = true` unconditionally. This is a permanent property of the PAS Policy â€” there is no upstream function to rescind clawback once enabled (see `/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/policy.move`). Operators who do not want their token to have clawback authority cannot express that intent through the Hadron API.
impact: Design rigidity. A non-clawback PAS asset cannot be registered via Hadron.
recommendation: Either expose `clawback_allowed: bool` as a parameter on `register_pas_asset`, or document explicitly in the module-level doc-comment that Hadron-deployed PAS assets are ALWAYS clawback-enabled and why (e.g. regulatory requirement).

title: token_template_pas hardcodes module 0x0::token_template â€” OTW collision across deploys
description: Both `token_template/` (regulated) and `token_template_pas/` (PAS) use `module 0x0::token_template` and `public struct TOKEN_TEMPLATE has drop {}`. OTW uniqueness is per-package, so independent publications of the same module are fine. However, both templates share the identical bytecode constants layout (SYMBOL/NAME/ICON_URL/DECIMALS/DESCRIPTION) â€” only the `make_regulated` vs non-regulated step differs. Consolidating into one template with a flag would reduce drift risk (only one hex string to keep patched).
impact: Maintenance and patch-consistency risk. The patcher `patchConstants` is shared â€” if regulated and PAS templates' bytecode diverge silently, one patch could corrupt the other.
recommendation: Either (a) unify the two templates into one, gating `make_regulated` on a boolean constant so the bytecode layout is identical, or (b) add a CI check that regenerates both hex strings from the compiled artifacts and fails on manual drift.

title: deployPasTokenTemplate: BYTECODE_HEX is hand-pasted with no regeneration recipe
description: The bytecode constant is a hand-pasted hex blob with no header comment describing how to regenerate it from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. Any change to the template source requires a manual rebuild + re-pasting. There is no CI check that the bytecode hex matches the compiled module.
impact: Silent drift between `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` and the bytecode the scripts actually publish.
recommendation: Add a top-of-file comment documenting the exact build command to regenerate the bytecode (e.g. `cd token_template_pas && sui move build && xxd -p build/token_template_pas/bytecode_modules/token_template.mv`). Consider a `bun run regenerate-bytecode` script and a CI check.
